metadata.title: VocabStudy: A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocabularies using a mobile phone application

abstract: The current work presents VocabStudy, a collection of natural language samples and children's vocabularies collected remotely by parents via a mobile phone application. The corpus contains 567,003 word tokens and represents 144 hours of speech over a period of six months from the language environment of 63 British toddlers aged 13 to 28 months. The corpus incorporates labeled speech samples of five typical routines: mealtime, bedtime, playtime, bathtime, and nappytime (i.e., diaper). To explore consistency and variability across these five linguistic contexts, topic modeling was employed. The topic most successfully detected as having a unique structure was mealtime, which was identified as such nearly 100% of the time; bathtime, nappytime, and bedtime were found to cluster together most of the time, suggesting that they have a similar language structure; playtime was correctly identified as such about 14% of the time. To validate the accuracy of parents marking the words that their child produced, the child's utterances found in the audio recordings were examined. About 18% of the vocabulary reported by parents appeared in the transcripts, and the reported vocabulary sizes were highly correlated with the number of unique words uttered by the children (ρ = .72, p < 001). The results suggest that most parents marked the words soon after their children start producing them (p < .001, d = 0.9). I discuss the advantages and disadvantages of using a mobile phone application as a method to collect children's data remotely, what worked to keep participants entering data, and what could have been done to avoid some issues encountered.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The ecological validity of the evaluations of children's abilities can be negatively affected by assessments conducted in structured environments that are far from familiar to the children. Naturalistic samples not only provide a better picture of children's competences but also help the delivery of better-quality support in real-world settings (e.g., see Bacon et al., 2019). Since parent-implemented language interventions have a significant influence on children's language abilities (Roberts & Kaiser, 2011), parents' behavioral patterns can also be evaluated for tailoring of interventions. Therefore, research should focus more on the identification and study of critical parental behaviors that are exhibited in their daily activities. Efforts have been made to investigate some natural language interactions taking place at home; however, apart from some daylong recordings, very few parental speech corpora capture the wide range of routines that toddlers experience every day (e.g., Glas et al. 2018;Tamis-LeMonda et al., 2018). Further, to our knowledge, no study has compared the semantic structure of the language used in different children's routines using a topic modeling algorithm to investigate their similarities and differences. Identifying routines or contexts that have a distinct and unique semantic structure can inform us about the best scenarios for word learning in early childhood.

The parental speech corpus that I present here, named VocabStudy (Jiménez & Hills, 2022), is composed of a range of labeled ordinary routines, namely mealtime, bedtime, nappytime, bathtime, and playtime. In this study, parents were specifically instructed to record these routines to ensure that the speech corpus included a balanced range of activities that children experience early in life. The data depict the 1 3 development of child-directed speech delivered at home over a six-month period, alongside vocabulary checklists that parents updated during the study. In addition to the Jiménez and Hills study, the current study provides a detailed description of the dataset collected, as well as a set of analyses to explore the structure of the speech data and to validate the vocabulary data.

The quality and characteristics of the VocabStudy data derive from the methodology employed. Jiménez and Hills (2022) sought to use a data recording device that (1) allowed remote participation; (2) allowed the collection of audio data and the recording of vocabulary updates; (3) was noninvasive, encouraging the recording of naturalistic interactions; (4) participants already owned, meaning no extra cost for researchers; (5) had a familiar interface with high ease of usage; (6) did not require the researcher to be present at the time of the recording (e.g., researcher over a videocall); ( 7) could be carried anywhere, and (8) allowed data entry at any time to offer flexible participation. With these requirements met, a high volume of participants become available, since little assistance is needed from the researcher. Mobile phones and the use of a specially designed software application are all that is required. Mobile phones have become everyday devices that most people carry with them most of their waking time, which make them a perfect instrument to record spontaneous behaviors whenever and wherever they happen. In addition, mobile phones are minimally obtrusive devices, as children are generally accustomed to their presence in everyday life.

Remote participation has become increasingly popular, especially after the COVID pandemic. The general thought is that the quality of the data might be jeopardized by carrying out research remotely instead of in person. In the field of natural language sampling, work by Manning et al. (2020) proved that this is not the case for videocalls aiming to measure children's language. When assessing children's language over a videocall, the language samples exhibited high reliability and validity, and were perfectly comparable to inlab language samples. The remote participation in Jiménez and Hills' study allowed the recruitment of a satisfactory sample of late-talking toddlers, the population of interest in the aforementioned study. Late talkers are a small population with a prevalence of 10-20% (Desmarais et al., 2008;Klee et al., 1998;Reilly et al., 2007). The opportunity to participate remotely meant that children from throughout the UK were automatically eligible to participate, increasing the chance to include hard-to-recruit populations such as late-talking toddlers.

VocabStudy was the name given to the Jiménez and Hills study (2022) during the recruitment process. The main purpose of collecting the VocabStudy data was to examine the relationship between early language delay and the language environment. The study focused on one particular aspect of quality of child-directed speech: its semantic richness. To investigate this link, it was necessary to collect longitudinal data consisting of samples of child-directed speech alongside the vocabularies of the children who experienced that speech. Jiménez and Hills' (2022) study provided further evidence of the link between the semantic quality of parental speech and the vocabulary skills of children. In this study, the parents of typical talkers showed the richest speech, followed by parents of late bloomers (i.e., children that were identified as late talkers at the beginning of the study but experienced a vocabulary spurt and became typical talkers by the end of the study), and late talkers' parents showed the least semantically rich speech.

VocabStudy followed and has been informed by previous work on language development. The association between the quality of the language input and later language outcomes is indeed a well-studied domain, where language quality has been measured in different ways. For example, many studies have considered number of word types (e.g., Thomas & Knowland, 2014: types of regular verbs and types of irregular verbs; Rowe, 2012: number of word types and number of rare words; Newman et al., 2016: number of word types: Huttenlocher et al., 2010: number of word types), others examined the diversity of verb frames (e.g. Hsu et al., 2017), some research considered a broader perspective and looked into discourse and pragmatic functions of parental speech (Vigil et al., 2005), and other studies focused on the diversity of the syntactic environments of the words (e.g., Huttenlocher et al., 2010;Naigles & Hoff-Ginsberg, 1998). Despite the evident contribution of quantity in parental speech on children's language development (e.g., Hart & Risley, 1995), quality has been found to have a stronger effect on children's language output (Anderson et al., 2021). Jiménez and Hills measured language quality by examining the diversity of the word's linguistic context, this is, those words that co-occur with the word in question within a window frame of five words in the speech stream. However, other frame types have been investigated in the language development literature, such as verb frames (Hsu et al., 2017), words adjacent to familiar words (Bortfeld et al., 2005), and frequent frames or co-occurrence patterns of words in speech (Mintz, 2003). The current study aims to further evaluate the quality and diversity of five important contexts in infancy, framed as routines, by employing a novel topic modeling algorithm. This novel approach in the language development literature could assist in revealing similarities and differences in the parental language structure by analyzing word patterns across routines.

The VocabStudy parental speech corpus is characterized by the inclusion of a balanced range of activities classified into routines, all captured at home with no restriction on the number of people present during the activity. VocabStudy has been inspired by studies contained within the CHILDES corpus, home for many important speech collections consisting of a large range of everyday routines (MacWhinney, 2000). Unlike VocabStudy, these corpora are largely cross-sectional. Further, many of the studies that investigated parental verbal behavior focused on one, two, or three activities or routines (e.g., Adi-Bensaid et al., 2022: playtime and mealtime;Raikes et al., 2006: mealtime;Majorano et al., 2013: playtime;Flynn & Masur, 2007: playtime and bathtime;Bates et al., 1988: free play, story reading and snack). Some researchers who attempted to cover many activities failed to record a comprehensive range of activities either because parents were free to include the activities they wanted in the recordings or because the time of the day in which the recordings were made naturally excluded some activities (e.g., Glas et al., 2018;Butler et al., 2022). A study by Tamis-LeMonda et al. (2018), which captured the parental speech experienced by forty 13-month-old infants at home, found it challenging to record grooming activities, as they were infrequent during the visits. Also, the study only allowed the mother and the baby present during the recordings, which narrowed the types of natural interactions that babies experience at home. Daylong recordings are particularly useful to avoid these issues whilst capturing a wide range of activities, with many important contributions to literature hosted in HomeBank, an online repository specifically designed to store daylong audio and transcription data recorded in natural environments (VanDam et al., 2016). However, daylong recordings can be very costly to transcribe. Taking these concerns into account, Jiménez and Hills employed an approach that (1) allowed the inclusion of a significant range of activities developed in a natural environment throughout the day, (2) made sure that some important activities were recorded, and (3) avoided daylong recordings to reduce the costs associated with transcription. To this end, the authors targeted five important routines, and offered flexibility to parents as to when to record these activities.

Capturing the speech around routines or activities seems important, given the large number of studies that have reported differences in parental speech across children's activities (see Tamis-LeMonda et al., 2018, for a review). That is, one cannot evaluate a child's language environment without considering most of the variation within it, mainly because language development is highly linked to routines (see for example script theory and interaction formats; Lucariello & Nelson, 1985;Bruner, 1985). In relation to this idea, recent research by Tamis-LeMonda et al. found that some infants' activities contained significantly more word tokens than others, and that activities were composed of different semantic content, identifying words that were activity-specific for each context (activities studied: feeding, grooming, book-sharing, object play, and transition). Lexical diversity across the activities was examined but no statistical analysis was performed.

Alongside audio recordings, parents in the VocabStudy were required to update a vocabulary checklist. The use of audio recordings of children's spontaneous speech is a common practice for validation purposes in the development of vocabulary checklists (e.g., Bates et al., 1988: English;Alcock et al., 2015: Kenyan languages;Gatt, 2007: Maltese). In general, studies have found a good correspondence between the vocabulary produced by children and the vocabularies reported by parents. For longitudinal studies, researchers typically ask parents to complete the same vocabulary checklist at various points in time (e.g., Wehberg et al., 2007). VocabStudy is novel in that it gave parents the freedom to enter words in the checklist at any time. Despite the advantages of this flexibility, this approach might compromise the validation of the vocabulary reported, as parents may not enter vocabulary promptly or accurately in the absence of pressure from the researcher to update the checklist. Therefore, the current study aims to validate both the promptness and the accuracy of the reported vocabularies collected.

Naturalistic samples not only provide a better picture of children's competences but also help the delivery of better-quality support in real-world settings (e.g., see Bacon et al., 2019). Since parent-implemented language interventions have a significant influence on children's language abilities (Roberts & Kaiser, 2011), parents' behavioral patterns can also be evaluated for tailoring of interventions.
sections.preview_tail: For longitudinal studies, researchers typically ask parents to complete the same vocabulary checklist at various points in time (e.g., Wehberg et al., 2007). VocabStudy is novel in that it gave parents the freedom to enter words in the checklist at any time. Despite the advantages of this flexibility, this approach might compromise the validation of the vocabulary reported, as parents may not enter vocabulary promptly or accurately in the absence of pressure from the researcher to update the checklist. Therefore, the current study aims to validate both the promptness and the accuracy of the reported vocabularies collected.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The aim of the present study is fourfold: (1) to describe the structure of the dataset, documenting the details of data collection and the study phone app, (2) to identify the main challenges of remote participation through a mobile app and how they were dealt with; (3) to explore the speech data to identify similarities and differences across the five routines recorded, and (4) to provide validation for the vocabulary data collected.

I previously underscored the way in which data are structured around routine-like topics. The current work sought to investigate whether the language structure of these linguistic contexts is also distinct or whether these contexts share some similarities in the word patterns used in them. To explore this, I employed topic modeling. Tamis-LeMonda et al. (2018) found that activities differed in their semantic composition, and identified the most prevalent word semantic categories for each activity. Given this finding, the prediction for the current research is that topic models will detect five semantic clusters of recordings corresponding with each routine. I chose topic modeling for two reasons. First, it allows the exposure of hidden structure in speech samples and it is able to identify speech samples that are similar in structure. Since this is an unsupervised technique, it avoids any topic preempting, meaning that the naïve model just works with bare language structures to identify the common topic across the samples. Second, I saw the opportunity to explore this novel and underused tool in the language development field to explore the complexity of the natural language, with the hope of inspiring future research.

I also aimed to validate the accuracy with which parents marked words in the vocabulary checklist. To do this, I made use of the words that children uttered during the recordings. Although a few language samples are unlikely to capture all of the words that a child can produce, we can evaluate the correspondence between what parents report and what children say. To do this, I conducted correlational analysis to check whether the number of words that parents reported were associated with the number of those reported words that the children uttered during the recordings. In addition, I investigated the promptness with which parents entered the words in the app after hearing their child saying them for the first time. To do this, I focused on the differences between the day the word was marked in the checklist and the day such word was uttered by the child for the first time in the audio recordings. I also report a useful series of descriptive statistics, such as the number of words that overlap between audios and checklists or the percentage increase in vocabulary when the audio-recorded words were taken into account. In the following sections, I describe the VocabStudy dataset and how the data were collected, followed by the speech structure exploration and the vocabulary validation results derived from the procedures mentioned above.

paragraphs: VocabStudy data comprise two datasets: a corpus of 144 hours of speech experienced by 63 British English-speaking toddlers at home, and a dataset with their vocabularies recorded at various points during the study. Both of these datasets were recorded by the caregivers and sent to the main researcher through a mobile app. All data are available at https:// github. com/ EvaJi menez/ LT_ TT_ LB_ data. In the next sections, I describe how data were collected, followed by the characteristics of the corpus, the vocabulary data, and the children and families.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: Ethical permission was sought and granted by the University of Warwick's Humanities & Social Sciences Research Ethics Committee to collect the present data for the study titled "Network analysis of growing vocabularies: A longitudinal study of late and typical talkers." Most families were recruited through a database at the baby laboratory at the University of Warwick, and some were recruited through advertisements posted in parental groups on Facebook, UKbased parental websites, and nurseries in the West Midlands, UK. Although we welcomed young children with any language ability into our study, we placed special emphasis on the participation of late-talking toddlers.

The study duration was six months. Parents were instructed to download a specially designed applicationfoot_0 onto their smartphones or tablets for the study. There were two versions of the app, one for Android devices and another for iOS devices. To start using the app, the parents were given a user name and password and a visual guide to using the app (see the Android version of the visual guide in the Supplemental Materials). The app consisted of a searchable vocabulary checklist of 681 words (see Children's vocabularies section for more details) and a voice recorder (Fig. 1; see also the visual guide in the Supplemental Materials). Parents were asked at the beginning of the study to mark all the words that their child either "speaks and understands" or just "understands." They were asked to update this checklist regularly; we made it clear that, ideally, we would like them to mark new words as soon as they noticed them (i.e., mark the new word whenever their child uttered or understood the word for the first time). Parents had access to the study's website, where they were asked to visit to download and read the guide document on how to use the phone application and a guide document on how to proceed to mark words and record audios.

Along with the marking of the child's vocabulary, parents were instructed to record audios of themselves using the study app while interacting with their children during different daily routines. They were asked to keep their phones close and to have no radio or TV on, and avoid external sources of noise as much as possible during recordings. Since we sought to capture the natural language surrounding the daily routines, we allowed any caregiver or sibling to appear in the recordings. Every two weeks, participants were sent an email reminding them to submit an audio recording. This is, parents were expected to submit one audio recording every two weeks until the end of the study. We specified in the email the routine or topic to be carried outfoot_1 for that particular week, and some guidance on what types of things could be recorded. In total, they were asked to submit two audio recordings for mealtime, bedtime, bathtime, and nappy/potty time, and four audio recordings of playtime. Topics were spread evenly throughout the study so that the time elapsed between any two recordings of the same topic was equal, with the exception of playtime, where the time elapsed between audios were shorter. We asked for a greater number of playtime recordings than other types, because the topics covered within play can be more volatile, and we sought to capture a wide range of games (e.g., cars, dolls, ball games, cooking). The guidance given to parents for making audio recordings can be found in the Supplemental Materials.

All audio files were transcribed by a professional UKbased transcription company. Due to the high cost of transcribing audio data, we selected only two audios of playtimefoot_2 per family for transcription. All the other audio files were transcribed. Before providing the audios to the transcription company, we further processed the audio files to cut out all the periods of silence by using Audacity, an opensource digital audio editor and recording application software. An average 37.3% (SD = 18.7%) of audio duration was deleted after this procedure. A total of 497 audio files were transcribed. The app sent the word updates to an Amazon Web Services-hosted server, and the audio files were sent to a secure server managed by the University of Warwick.

The original duration of the audio recordings was found to differ significantly across the five topics (χ 2 (4) = 16.972, p = .002). A post hoc analysis revealed differences between bathtime (M = 1362.207 seconds) and bedtime (M = 1110.884 seconds), and between bathtime and nappytime (M = 1109.989 seconds). The time recorded by parents was similar across the audios sent during the study, i.e., early recordings had a similar length as late recordings (χ 2 (11) = 10.788, p = .46). A dataset with the original duration of the audio recordings can be found at https:// github. com/ EvaJi menez/ LT_ TT_ LB_ data (see "audio_duration.csv").

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: Out of the 129 families that started the study, 15 families dropped out. Although this represents a low level of attrition, the frequency and the timing of submitting data through the app was varied. Parents were not always diligent in submitting audio recordings on time. If they took more than two weeks to submit the required audio, they were reminded through email to do so. During the study, the families received two children's books every month of participation and at the end of the study, and a semantic network visualization of their children's vocabularies as they grew throughout the study, one graph per month of participation. In many instances, when the families received their monthly reward through post (i.e., the children's books), they immediately submitted any overdue audio recordings and/or updated their children's vocabularies. Therefore, rewarding participation at various points throughout the study was helpful in keeping parents entering data in the app. Nonetheless, although parents were specifically instructed to mark the new words that their child produced or understood as soon as they noticed it, the general tendency was to report words in batches.

A study website was set up to assist parents during the study. The study webpage was a sub-page of the Department of Psychology site of the University of Warwick. On the webpage, parents could access the guidelines for the installation and use of the study app on their Android or Apple devices, and access a guide on how to record the requested topics. When parents had problems submitting audio files through the app, they were instructed to submit them manually to the study website. The participants were also asked to complete the two forms available on the study website that they needed to complete during the study (see Additional data: Understanding and gestures section). The app and the website shared the same study name (i.e., Vocab-Study) and logo (i.e., a little girl talking on the phone), to help the participant recognize that both platforms related to the same study. Having a website supporting the study app guaranteed that any information was submitted through a secure medium, and that participants had access to any information regarding the study at all times. That only few Fig. 1 The interface of the VocabStudy app for Android devices parents needed substantial assistance via email suggests that parents generally did not have issues finding, downloading, and using the study app.

Having a website supporting the study app guaranteed that any information was submitted through a secure medium, and that participants had access to any information regarding the study at all times. That only few Fig. 1 The interface of the VocabStudy app for Android devices parents needed substantial assistance via email suggests that parents generally did not have issues finding, downloading, and using the study app.
sections.preview_head: Out of the 129 families that started the study, 15 families dropped out. Although this represents a low level of attrition, the frequency and the timing of submitting data through the app was varied. Parents were not always diligent in submitting audio recordings on time. If they took more than two weeks to submit the required audio, they were reminded through email to do so.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: One hundred fourteen British families completed the study. Fifty-one families were excluded for the following reasons (and therefore their audio recordings were not transcribed): their children had a major medical problem, suffered a prolonged or recurrent ear infection, received a diagnosis of developmental disability, had visual or hearing impairment, lived in a multilingual home, produced a number of words at the end of the study not larger than the largest vocabulary size identified in the late talker group (see the Characteristics of the children and their families section), the duration of their participation in the study was less than three months, or they covered less than three topics in the audio recordings. The dataset that I share here is composed of the 63 families that were not excluded. The remainder of this article will focus on the data for these 63 families.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The participant families took part in the study at different points between 2017 and 2019. The corpus has a total of 497 transcriptions covering five different topics related to typical routines that young children experience every day at home: 92 bath times, 86 bedtimes, 92 nappy times, 95 mealtimes, and 132 play times, all in csv format (see Table 1 for an example). The corpus has a total of 567,003 word tokens and a total of 9858 word types. The transcribers were instructed to specify the gender of the speaker and whether it was a child or an adult. They were also asked to use the symbol [] for guesses of unintelligible utterances, () for comments, such as noises or when two utterances overlapped, and _____ for long pauses. Since there were several transcribers working on the transcriptions, they used different labels for the speakers to refer to the same person type, such as "Female" and "Female_1."

The different labels can be corrected (i.e., unique labels for the same person type) using the code I share for the analysis described in Consistency and variability across the topics section. Transcribers identified up to two females, up to two males, up to three children, and even one dog. The file names (e.g., "P_WWW_XX_Y_ZZZZ," see column Audio_file_name in Table 1) refer to the participant code ("P_WWW"), the age in months of the child at the time of the recording ("XX"), the order in which the audio was recorded ("Y," within each family), and topic ("ZZZZ"). Sometimes, parents intentionally sent audio recordings containing two topics to make up for a missed audio recording, in which case the second topic is also specified in the file name.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The vocabulary checklist is composed of 681 words that are either marked as "1" = understands, "2" = speaks and understands, or " " (i.e., empty cell) = does not speak or understand (see Table 2 for an example of one of the participants). All children have more than one vocabulary update, ranging from 4 to 113 (mean = 26.9, SD = 20.9). The 681 words were taken from a British adaptation of the MacArthur Child Development Inventory Words & Sentences (W&S CDI; Fenson et al., 1993) that a group of investigators created at the University of Lincoln, UK (Meints & Fletcher, 2001). The vocabulary data also includes the child's age when the vocabulary update was completed (column name "age" in Table 2), the order number in which the update was done ("data_entry_number"), and the day of the study when the update was done, with zero marking the first day of participation ("day_of_data_entry"). The average age in months of the participating children at the start of study was 18.2 (SD = 3.3, range = [13.0, 28.0]) and 24.0 at the end of the study (SD = 3.3, range = [18.5, 33.5]). The children produced an average of 29.9 words at the start of the study (SD = 114.6, range = [2, 223]) and 206.7 words at the end (SD = 114.6, range = [21,392]). With respect to their receptive vocabularies, the children understood an average of 99.9 words at the start of the study (SD = 94.1, range = [3, 460]) and 447.2 words at the end of the study (SD = 182.8,range = [50,903]). The variability of the reported vocabulary sizes is large due to the nature of the sample, as late-talking toddlers were targeted in the recruitment process to be compared to typical talkers (see more details in the following section). A summary the development of the children's vocabularies during the study is available at https:// github. com/ EvaJi menez/ LT_ TT_ LB_ data (see "summary_vocab.csv")

sections.header_raw: Characteristics of the children and their families
sections.header_norm: characteristics of the children and their families
paragraphs: The 63 participating children were aged between 13 and 28 months at the beginning of the study, and 28 were female (see previous section for more details about age of the participants; gender can be found in "summary_vocab.csv"; see paragraph above). As previously described, none of the children had a major medical problem, a problematic ear infection, or a developmental disability or impairment, and all were exposed to just one language at home. Most children were the only child (n = 40), 17 children had one sibling, five had two siblings, and one child had three or more siblings. Slightly more than half of the children attended nursery (n = 38), and 24 children were exposed to baby sign language, although 17 of these children did not use any sign to express themselves.

In the original study by Jiménez and Hills (2022), 22 children were identified as late talkers, since their productive vocabularies scored below the 20th percentile either in the W&S CDI vocabulary norms or in the Words and Gestures CDI (W&G CDI; Fenson et al., 1993), depending on the age of the child at the time of the identification (i.e., beginning or end of the study). Further, 22 children were labeled as late bloomers as they were delayed at the beginning of the study but caught up with their same-age peers at the end of the study (i.e., > 20th percentile when they finished participating). The rest of the participants (n = 19) scored above the 20th percentile, and were labeled as typical talkers. As can be seen in Fig. 2, by the end of the study, 45 children scored a greater percentile than at the beginning of the study, five children scored a lower percentile, and 13 children obtained the same percentile.

Table 3 shows the characteristics of the parents of the participating children. The percentage of mothers who had completed some university education is higher than that of the fathers (mother 85%; fathers 56%; "some university education" comprises "some university," "university degree," "some postgraduate work," and "postgraduate degree"). The most prevalent age range for mothers is 26-30 years and for fathers is 31-35 years. Most families (61.9%) had a household income of £45,000 or greater. No significant differences in family characteristics were identified between the three talker groups in the Jiménez and Hills study (2022; see article for more details).

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: Part of the CDI relates to children's understanding of regular expressions, communication with gestures, and early play skills. Participants were asked a series of these questions before they started the study, after three months of participation, and at the end of the study. Many parents did not Fig. 2 CDI percentiles of the 63 participating children at the start and the end of the study complete one or two of these three questionnaires, leaving a significant amount of missing data. Given that the data were not critical to identify late talkers in the sample and given that there was a significant amount of missing data, Jiménez and Hills did not include these data in the analysis (2022).

In any case, the Understanding and Gestures data are shared together with the VocabStudy data for their potential use in future studies. The Understanding and Gestures checklist (U&G) covers three questions about first signs of understanding (e.g., "respond when his or her name is called"), 18 questions about things children understand (e.g., "are you hungry?"), seven questions about games and routines (e.g., "play 'peekaboo'"), 18 questions about actions with objects (e.g., "eat with a spoon or fork"), 15 questions about imitating other adult actions ( e.g., "sweep with broom or mop"), and 12 questions about children pretending to be a parent ( e.g., "put to bed"). Out of the 63 families, 43 completed the G&U questionnaire three times as requested, 10 completed it twice, one completed it only once, and six completed it four times (where on one occasion they completed the questionnaire twice very close in time).

Out of the 63 families, 43 completed the G&U questionnaire three times as requested, 10 completed it twice, one completed it only once, and six completed it four times (where on one occasion they completed the questionnaire twice very close in time).

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The topic structure of the corpus data can be exploited to further examine similarities and differences in the language structure. Previous studies have found differences in the semantic composition of the language used in activities experienced by infants (e.g., Tamis-LeMonda et al., 2018). Therefore, the expectation is that the different routines recorded in Jimenez and Hills's study (2022) would contain different word patterns. However, there are reasons to believe that some topics might share a similar language structure in the current corpus. Recording audios around a particular topic can be tricky for parents. This was highlighted in the participant information sheet as one of the issues of taking part in the study: "Parents may feel uncomfortable prolonging an activity that has naturally ended in order to complete the suggested length of the audio recording. In this case, there is no need to continue with the activity, just carry on with a different activity that suits the moment (…). The interactions should be as natural as possible." This might have prompted some parents to overlap two or more topics within the same audio recording. Likewise, some activities occur in more than one topic. For example, brushing one's teeth can be identified as part of bedtime and also part of the bath routine. Also, some groups of words belonging to a semantic category can be present in two or more different activities, for example, reading a book about animals for bedtime, and playing with animal toys during playtime. Therefore, in this section I aim to test whether the language structure is similar or different across the five topics, and to examine these differences or commonalities.

One way to examine the language structure of the topics is to train a topic classification model. In the present work I use Top2Vec, one of the most recent algorithms for topic modeling and semantic search written in Python (Angelov, 2020). Top2Vec creates embedded document and word vectors. Each vector represents a document or word in a multidimensional space, and their proximity to each other can determine how semantically similar documents and words are amongst them. Jointly embedded word vectors are also generated to identify the keywords for each detected topic (i.e., the words that best define the topic). Keywords are the closest word vectors to a detected topic vector. Top2Vec is differentiated from other commonly used algorithms like Word2Vec in that it uses transformers instead of recurrent neural networks, a more efficient way to generate vector embeddings (for more details see Angelov, 2020). To find topic clusters, Top2Vec uses UMAP, a dimension reduction algorithm that generates lower-dimensional embedding of document vectors. In sum, the use of Top2Vec helps to expose the internal semantic structure of the speech samples and allows us to (1) cluster transcriptions with similar language patterns (i.e., clustered by topics) and (2) identify the keywords for each detected topic.

Topic modeling allows us to evaluate aspects related to the language experienced in early childhood. Specifically, it would be interesting to know which routines show structurally similar language. This could inform us about where contextual diversity comes from, which is an important word property known to facilitate word acquisition (e.g., Hills et al., 2010). Words with high contextual diversity are those that appear in multiple and different linguistic contexts. If the present study shows that nappytime shares a similar language structure as bathtime but a different structure from mealtime, one might reasonably argue that words that appear in both nappytime routines and mealtime routines have a greater chance of being high in contextual diversity than words that appear in nappytime routines and bathtime routines. Equally, topic modeling could single out the most distinctive routines in terms of their language structure, which might inform us about those topics that are most likely to introduce new word uses. At the same time, distinctive contexts have also been found to promote word learning as a result of the high predictability of the language patterns found in them (Bruner, 1985;Roy et al., 2015). I further discuss these contextual aspects in relation to the results in the discussion section. I carried out different sets of analyses to answer two questions:

Q1: Does the language structure differ among the five topics? To answer this question, I tested whether the topic model could accurately detect the five topics (i.e., five clusters of transcription files, regardless of the intended topicfoot_3 ). Q2: Which topics have the most distinct language structure? To answer this question, I conducted three types of analysis that (1) identified the topics that tend to co-occur with other topics, (2) tested to what extent transcriptions are classified correctly to their intended topic in the models, and (3) identified the topic or topics that have the highest percentage of correctly classified transcription files.

The main goal of this section is to provide information about the structural similarities and differences in the topics recorded in the speech data, where topic modeling is employed as a tool to meet this goal.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The first goal is to determine the number of clusters of transcriptions in the corpus. To do this, one could simply run Top2Vec over the corpus and count the number of topics detected by the model (henceforth, detected topics); however, due to the stochastic nature of the dimension reduction stage (see UMAP in the above section), a different number of topics are generated each time Top2Vec is run. For this reason, I generated 100 topic models and averaged the results. The second goal is to classify individual transcripts as belonging to one of the five specific events or routines (i.e., mealtime, playtime, bedtime, nappytime, and bathtime). However, to quantify the number of times that a transcript has been correctly classified, each detected topic needs to be first classified into one routine. For this task, the lists of keywords for each detected topic produced by Top2Vec were examined to determine the topic of the cluster/detected topic. This step was conducted in all 100 topic models generated. The summarized details of the workflow follow:

1. After cleaning the corpus, generate 100 Top2Vec topic models. Each topic model detects a specific number of clusters of transcripts (i.e., detected topics) and a list of 50 keywords per cluster. 2. Calculate the frequency of the top ten unique keywords generated for each detected topic. 3. Out of the most frequent keywords, manually identify the routine related to those words (e.g., bathtime for the keyword bath). Select the three most frequent keywords for each routine, henceforth routine keywords. 4. For each Top2Vec topic model, examine the list of keywords generated for each detected topic and check whether it contains one or more of the routine keywords, and if so, proceed to assign the detected topic with the respective routine-topic. The detected topics become classified topics (i.e., labeled topics). 5. Use these classified topics to generate a numeric value for each transcript based on which routines are assigned to it. This generates a numeric value indicating the extent to which a given transcript is classified as belonging to a specific routine type.

Prior to training the topic model, the data were cleaned of irrelevant material. Despite Top2Vec carrying out a preprocessing cleaning (e.g., removing stop words, stemming, and lemmatization), some elements such as squared brackets and their content, and some proper names were not removed by this preprocessing stage, and therefore the texts had to be manually cleaned.

After cleaning the data, 100 topic models were trained, and for each model, the number of topics detected and the top ten keywords for each detected topic were extracted (i.e., those words that had the highest score in belonging to a particular topic). This procedure yielded a list of 128 unique top keywords across the 100 models. In order to classify each detected topic into a routine, five sets of routine-related keywords were created. To do this, the most frequent keywords were examined, and for each routine (i.e., bedtime, bathtime, playtime, mealtime, nappytime), three keywords were manually selected by the researcher based on her best knowledge of what routine these keywords represented. The selection of some routine keywords was avoided as their topic was highly ambiguous, e.g., story (playtime or bedtime) or legs (bathtime or nappytime). The selected routine keywords were spoon, fork, and yummy for mealtime; sleep, bed, and bedroom for bedtime; towel, arms, and washed for bathtime; triangle, animal, lorry for playtime; and change, nappy, and bottom for nappytime. The full list of keywords and their frequency across the models can be seen in the Supplemental Materials.

Once the lists of routine keywords were created, the 100 topic models were re-examined, and each detected topic was classified into a routine. This procedure was conducted in the following way for each model: When the original list of the topic keywords for a detected topic contained one or more of the keywords from the list of routine keywords, the detected topic was classified into that routine. This means that a detected topic could also be classified into two or more routines. For example, for model #2, topic 1 was categorized into bedtime, bathtime, and nappytime because "sleep," "towel," and "nappy" were among the keywords, topic 2 was categorized into mealtime because it had "spoon" as a keyword, and topic 3 into playtime because it had "triangle" as a keyword (for the sake of simplicity, only some keywords are included in this example).

In the next step, each document was scored using the classified topics to determine how well the models detected their original intended topic/routine. In other words, if a parent recorded a mealtime event, we want to know whether that transcription was assigned to a detected topic that was classified into the mealtime routine. The scores given were as follows: 1 if the transcript was correctly assigned to a detected topic that was classified into one routine only (following the above example, a topic classified only into mealtime); 0.5 if correctly assigned to a detected topic that was classified into two different routines (mealtime and playtime); 0.25 if correctly assigned to a detected topic that was classified into three or more different routines (mealtime, playtime, and bedtime); and 0 if it had been incorrectly assigned to a detected topic that was classified into one or more different routines (playtime, or playtime and bathtime). Each of the 497 documents was scored 100 times (one per topic model). Then the document scores were regrouped by their original topic/routine, summed, and divided by the number of documents of their respective original topics, resulting in five total scores, one per topic. This approach allows documents to receive some score even when they belong to a multitopic cluster (i.e., 0.5 or 0.25). A second set of scores was created using a tougher penalization where only documents that were correctly classified into one routine only scored 1, and 0 otherwise. All code can be found in https:// github. com/ EvaJi menez/ LT_ TT_ LB_ data.

paragraphs: Most topic models detected two topics (n = 82), and only a few models detected three (n = 14) or four topics (n = 4). Table 4 shows the distribution of the routines across the detected topics. Bathtime, nappytime, and bedtime topics co-occurred in all 100 models, and these three topics co-occurred with playtime 81 times. Playtime co-occurred the most with bedtime (n = 86). Mealtime never co-occurred with any other topic, and playtime did not co-occur with any other topic in 14 of the models.

As can be seen in Fig. 3A, out of the 497 transcriptions, 89 were correctly assigned to a detected topic that was classified into the original topic/routine 100% of the time (score = 100; original topic in all the transcripts was mealtime), and 22 transcriptions were incorrectly classified in every model (score = 0; various original topics). Although the average score for the transcriptions is 38.9 (Mdn = 25), results are highly skewed (SD = 30.31), with most transcription scores ranging between 0 and 38. When aggregating the transcription scores by their original topic (Fig. 3B), we can see that mealtime obtained the highest score, with the other four topics scoring significantly lower. When considering a harder penalization (Fig. 3C), mealtime continues to show the highest score, followed by playtime, whose transcriptions were rarely correctly classified, and then nappytime, bathtime, and bedtime, whose transcriptions were never correctly classified.

Q1: Does the language structure of the five topics differ? I hypothesized in the introduction that the language structure of the five routines investigated in the current study would show distinct language patterns. However, none of the Top2Vec models detected five topics. Instead, most models detected two different topics, which suggests that some routines share a similar language structure. This result disagrees with previous findings that underscored significant differences in the semantic composition of the language used in different activities experienced by infants at home (Tamis-LeMonda et al., 2018). Analytical differences between the current study and that of Tamis-LeMonda et al. might explain the disagreement in the results. The latter study examined the proportion of word semantic categories within each activity, and compared their composition to the other activities, whereas the current research examines the repeating pattern of co-occurring terms in a corpus, with the expectation of identifying clusters of activities/transcripts with unique semantic structure. Furthermore, Tamis-LeMonda et al.'s semantic analysis only included content nouns and verbs, whereas the current study has made no word exclusion. Q2: Which topics have the most distinct language structure? The results indicate that only mealtime transcriptions shared a unique language structure. The types of words and the language structure used during mealtime recordings help to easily define the topic, and they seem to be sufficiently distinguishable from other routines that children experience in early life. Although some word patterns can occasionally identify playtime sessions, it seems that these patterns are also present during other routines, making it difficult to recognize clusters of pure playtime documents. This suggests that parents tend to include games and elements of play across different types of activities. The language structures used in bath, nappy, and bed routines are quite comparable. One thing about these routines is that they tend to overlap in time or occur very close in time. For example, before bedtime, having a bath and a nappy changed is particularly common. Also, toddlers have their nappy changed several times a day, which increases the probability of co-occurrence with other activities. Researchers seeking to exploit the topiclike structure of the VocabStudy corpus for a future study might want to further split bathtime, nappytime, and bedtime transcriptions to delimit when a particular topic starts and ends. The use of topic keywords can be particularly useful for this task. For example, identifying the word "nappy" in a bedtime transcription could signal the beginning of the nappy change, and then the researcher could set a number of words afterwards as a threshold to define the end of the nappy change routine. I further discuss in the general discussion why the topic models might have failed to identify different semantic structures for each routine.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: Testing the accuracy with which parents reported productive vocabularies is a difficult task. Nevertheless, the collected audio recordings can provide useful information about children's productive vocabularies: it can be tested whether the number of words reported by parents is correlated with the number of CDI words found in the audio recordings. If the numbers correlate with each other, it would suggest that parents were not randomly marking words, and that the remote marking of words can be said to approximate children's real vocabulary. Another important type of accuracy to measure is the promptness with which parents reported the words after hearing their child uttering them for the first time. If most of the child's produced words were marked by parents before appearing in an audio recording, it would suggest that parents were keeping up with the vocabulary updates. In addition, valuable information can be extracted from the audio recordings that complement the vocabularies reported by parents, such as words not marked or words uttered by the child that were not part of the vocabulary checklist provided. In the following section, I investigate the extent to which parents report with promptness and other important summary statistics comparing the vocabularies reported by parents and the audio recordings of children's utterances.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: After lemmatizing the utterances in the transcription, the words that the target child produced were extracted and compared against words that the parents reported. Items from the vocabulary checklist composed of two or more words (e.g., "so big!") or those that are not common nouns per se (e.g., "pet's name") were not included in the calculations. Verbal imitation of adults' utterances are common early on (e.g., Masur, 1995;Snow, 1981). However, these instances do not guarantee that children know the meaning of the words in their vocabulary, which is why parents were instructed to mark only spontaneous utterances. To correct for this, the same procedure was conducted but this time the child's words were not considered as produced when the same word/words were also produced by the speaker in the preceding utterance. A series of simple descriptive analysis and correlation analysis was performed to compare checklists and children´s utterances in the transcriptions (see Table 5).

The day children uttered a CDI word was subtracted from the day parents entered that word in the checklist (i.e., "day_ of_data_entry" in Table 2; these can be either positive or negative integers). Not all words marked by parents appeared in the audio recordings, and vice versa. Therefore, only CDI one-word phrases that appeared in both checklists and audio recordings were included in the analysis. Also, only spontaneous words found in audio recordings were considered. Within families, each unique CDI word was given two scores: a "checklist score" and an "audio recording score." If a word was marked in the checklist before appearing in an audio recording, then a 1 was given to the checklist score, and otherwise a 0. If a word was uttered by the child in an audio recording before having been marked in the checklist, then a 1 was given to audio recording score, otherwise a 0. If the child did not utter a word, both checklist score and audio recording scores for that word were given 0. A total score was calculated for each family by summing each score type separately and dividing that number by the number of words for that family. A non-parametric test was performed to compare these two scores and examine whether parents were entering words promptly.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: Table 5 shows summary statisticsfoot_4 that contrast in different ways the words produced by children in audio recordings with words reported by parents. Each calculation was carried out in two ways: considering all the utterances that children spoke during the audio recordings, and considering only the spontaneous utterances (see previous section for more details). On average, nearly a fifth of the words marked in the vocabulary reports are uttered by the children during the audio recordings. Figure 4 suggests that most parents failed to report between 1 and 15 CDI words. However, this number seems to be worrisome for some participants: about nine families failed to report more than 20 CDI words. A closer inspection of these families revealed that seven of them had reported large vocabulary sizes (vocabulary sizes = 320, 235, 206, 244, 318, 108, 309, 253), and two of them had small to medium vocabulary sizes (vocabulary sizes = 39, 62). Vocabulary checklists can be corrected taking into account the CDI words uttered by children in the recording, which can increase CDI vocabulary size by up to 20% on average when considering spontaneous and non-spontaneous words, and up to 8% when considering only spontaneous words. In general, a substantial number of words that children utter during the recordings are not part of the CDI. The consideration of CDI and non-CDI words, as well as spontaneous and non-spontaneous utterances, meant an average increase of 58% in the reported vocabulary size, and an average of 21% when including spontaneous words onlyfoot_5 (see more details in Table 5: Percentage increase in vocabulary size after including words identified in recordings).

The number of words reported by parents is highly positively correlated with the number of those reported words uttered in the audio recordings (all words: ρ = .72, p < .001; spontaneous words: ρ = .69, p < .001), and moderately positively correlated with the total number of CDI and non-CDI words uttered by children (all words: ρ = . 50, p < .001; spontaneous words: ρ = .53, p < .001). The number of reported words was not correlated with number of unreported CDI words in the audio recordings (all words: ρ = .02, p > .05; spontaneous words: ρ = .10, p > .05).

Fifty-nine children uttered reported CDI words during audio recordings. Out of 1328 CDI words analyzed across families (i.e., summing across children all unique words reported and uttered by each child), 939 were reported before being audio-recorded (henceforth checklist-found words), 389 words were first audio-recorded and then marked by parents (audio-found words), and 43 words were reported the same day the child uttered the word during an audio recording. Audio recording scores (i.e., scores that measure to what extent words were first audio-recorded and then subsequently reported) were significantly lower (Mdn = 5) than the checklist scores (i.e., scores that measure to what extent words were first reported and then audio-recorded; Mdn = 14), U = 2583.5, p < .001, large effect size: η 2 = 0.174, d = 0.9. However, an inspection by family reveals that five families had more audio-found words than checklist-found words. Forty-eight families had more checklist-found words than audio-found words, and six families had an equal number of checklist-found words and audio-found words.

The results suggest that parents were not randomly marking words, and that the words they reported are possibly a satisfactory approximation of their child's real CDI vocabulary. After controlling for potential utterances where children were just repeating after the adults, some families failed to mark a substantial number of CDI words during the study. Tracking children with large vocabularies can be challenging and demanding. Yet, no correlation was found between the number of words reported (i.e., reported vocabulary size of child) and number of missed CDI words. The use of the children's utterances in the transcriptions can increase the size and the accuracy of parental vocabulary reports considerably. Regarding promptness in reporting words, the result suggests that most parents tend not to delay marking the words recently produced by their children, although a few families seemed to have tracked their children's vocabularies with some significant tardiness.

It should be noted that the procedure followed to measure spontaneous utterances is a very conservative way to identify CDI and non-CDI words from the recordings. In the current study, about 50% of the children's utterances are repetitions (see Number of words uttered by the child in recordings in Table 2), whereas previous studies reported that about 20% of similar-age-children's utterances are repetitions (Casla et al., 2022;Che et al., 2018;Snow, 1981;Užgiris et al., 1989). However, the current study calculates the number of unique words, not the number of word tokens repeated after parents. The correlation between the number of reported CDI words and the number of words produced by the child in the recordings was higher when not taking into account all potential repetitions. This result has two interpretations: either that children were demonstrating that they knew the words and so they were also able to say them spontaneously, or that parents considered these repetitions as real word production and marked them erroneously. For this reason, I am unable to identify which of the two approaches of measuring production vocabulary from the recording is the best. In any case, the same conclusion can be made by using either approach: that parents' measures of children's production are reliable, even when conservative measures of vocabularies are considered.

Table 5 shows summary statisticsfoot_4 that contrast in different ways the words produced by children in audio recordings with words reported by parents. Each calculation was carried out in two ways: considering all the utterances that children spoke during the audio recordings, and considering only the spontaneous utterances (see previous section for more details). On average, nearly a fifth of the words marked in the vocabulary reports are uttered by the children during the audio recordings. Figure 4 suggests that most parents failed to report between 1 and 15 CDI words. However, this number seems to be worrisome for some participants: about nine families failed to report more than 20 CDI words. A closer inspection of these families revealed that seven of them had reported large vocabulary sizes (vocabulary sizes = 320, 235, 206, 244, 318, 108, 309, 253), and two of them had small to medium vocabulary sizes (vocabulary sizes = 39, 62). sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The current study makes three main contributions: first, it presents an unusual but effective method to collect speech and vocabulary data remotely; second, it shares a rare large, longitudinal dataset structured around routines experienced by toddlers, along with their vocabularies; and third, the semantic structure of the speech data has been analyzed using an unsupervised machine learning technique, a novel approach for the purpose of exploring linguistic structures in child-directed speech. I demonstrated that the employment of a mobile phone app by participants can be useful for remote data collection in developmental studies, particularly for collecting natural language samples. Amongst the benefits were the high number of participants who signed up despite the characteristics of the study (e.g., six-month commitment), the large amount of longitudinal data collected, the relatively lower cost compared to other options (e.g., buying audio recorders), and the lower level of engagement needed by the researcherfoot_6 . The low attrition seems to be related to the monthly reward system for taking part in the study. Generally, parents found it easy to participate through the app, and with the right written support (i.e., guidelines found on the study website), little assistance was needed.

The use of the VocabStudy app yielded a collection of natural language samples featuring five important routines that young British children experienced at home every day. Results obtained from topic modeling suggest that the language structure found in mealtime transcriptions are semantically unique, and should be easily discriminated from other topics. On the other hand, audio recordings intending to capture bathtime, nappytime, and bedtime routines shared a similar language structure. Some models were able to pick up playtime routines, demonstrating that playtime can have a unique language structure potentially representing the typical dyadic communication happening in children's games. However, playtime routines were often found to co-occur with bedtime, bathtime, and nappytime routines. This finding suggests that play activities might occur while other routines are developing. There are two potential explanations for these results, one related to the language environments, and the other to the methodology used. Parents might have utilized the same language patterns across these topics, may have tended to overlap topics within transcriptions, and/or might have included decontextualized talk about events and ideas unrelated to the task at hand. Another explanation is that the routine keywords selected to identify topic clusters are not sufficiently representative of the topic, and instead other not-so-obvious keywords should have been selected. However, the majority of the topic models detected only two clusters of transcripts, meaning that when the routine keywords were selected, they were already representative words of a cluster of transcripts of different original topics. The fact that the models failed to detect five clusters of transcripts, regardless of their original topic, is already telling us that not all the routines will be represented in the models, no matter how well the routine keyword selection is done. Therefore, these results might derive from one or more factors related to parental speech (e.g., overlapping sets of words across routines) and/or related to parents failing to follow the protocol (e.g., recording two routines in the same recording, deliberately or accidentally). Although this study failed to determine these factors, I encourage future research to investigate the origin of the structural similarities between these routines in the VocabStudy dataset.

Words that appear in mealtime routines might benefit from the uniqueness of their semantic structure. On one hand, the distinct linguistic context of mealtime situations compared to other routines allows words to be surrounded by a more consistent background of words, which might facilitate the learning of their meaning. Bruner posited that "interaction formats," or activities with highly predictable patterns of words, were important in word acquisition. Roy et al. (2015) tested this idea and showed that words that are produced earlier were found in more distinctive contexts. On the other hand, words that are high in contextual diversity are also acquired earlier on (e.g., Hills et al., 2010;Jiménez & Hills, 2022). It might be the case that two ingredients are needed for words to be learned faster: to appear in consistent contexts AND in many different contexts. For example, the learning of the word water might be facilitated by appearing frequently during mealtime, which is a highly consistent routine, and by appearing in other routines at the same time, where it has the opportunity to co-occur with other different words, increasing the chance of learning new nuances of its meaning (e.g., water to clean in bathtime, but to drink in mealtime). Future research could investigate this hypothesis using the VocabStudy dataset to shed light on how diversity and distinctiveness play an important role in word acquisition, even though these are opposite contextual measures.

Regarding productive vocabularies, parents seemed to have logged a good estimation of what their children could say and were generally tracking their vocabularies on time. However, some parents failed to mark a significant number of words that should have been marked in the vocabulary checklist provided. Although large vocabularies can be tricky to track, no correlation was found between children's CDI vocabulary size and the number of words that parents failed to mark in the app. This means that some parents tended to lose track of a number of words regardless of how many words their children knew. What remains unknown is whether these unreported words share some properties that makes them more difficult to track, e.g., they are infrequent words. Future research using the VocabStudy vocabulary dataset might benefit from adding the words that the child uttered during the audio recordings to complete their vocabulary checklist and improve the accuracy of these lists. Further, future research concerned with the accuracy with which parents record words promptly could remove those problematic families identified in this work from their work dataset.

There are several potential uses of the VocabStudy data. One could exploit the routine-like structure of the corpus. For exampotential use would be to explore the semantic richness of the routines. Jiménez and Hills (2022) considered the context of a word as the word that immediately surround the word in speech. For example, the linguistic context of the word baby in "look, the tiny baby sleeps in the crib" would be tiny and sleeps in a context of window size 1. A different and valid approach would be to consider the word context at much larger scale, such as the routine where the word in encountered. Compared with the above approach, it would be like the window size was as large as the words contained in the audio recording. In other words, the transcription/document would be the linguistic context. The consideration of a document as a word's context is an approach largely used in studies exploring the effects of semantic diversity on various cognitive tasks (Hoffman et al., 2013;Hsiao & Nation, 2018;Pagán et al., 2020; see also chapter 5 in Jiménez Mesa, 2020). On the other hand, one could make use of the language abilities of children in the corpus. Jiménez and Hills (2022) identified three language groups: 19 typical talkers, 22 late talkers, and 22 late bloomers. The presence of children with different language skills in the sample and the fact that their vocabularies have been longitudinally tracked offers a possibility to further explore the relation between language environment and early language acquisition. For example, studies could explore the different quality aspects and frame types typically investigated in the literature to expose which ones could be particularly critical for a typical language development.

Given the naturalistic character of the language sample, typical interactions between children and adults can be analyzed from different perspectives. For example, one could investigate individual differences in the type of routines these children experience and how these differences relate to language attainments. Also, future research could investigate whether some activities developed during specific routines elicit more utterances than others or to what extent they contribute to the production of new words in children. Some studies could concentrate on the comparison of the language structure of dyadic interactions compared to triadic interactions (or more people), since the number of speakers varied across transcriptions. The presence of other adults and other children in the transcriptions can also be exploited in research where the type of speaker interacting with the child is the focus of interest.

sections.paper_id: Jiménez - 2023 - VocabStudy A remote collection of naturalistic topic-structured parental speech and toddlers’ vocab
paragraphs: The present work has a number of limitations. As mentioned above, parents generally failed to mark some words. A potential solution for this in future research would be to set the app to randomly ask parents whether their child knows some particular words. For example, they could be words that the child still does not produce but that, according to normative data, a child of the same age generally would. Another issue reported in this paper is that parents tend to report words in batches. Reporting individual words can be demanding. Perhaps the above solution of sending questions to parents through the app would encourage parents to enter data more often. The current research cannot confirm whether parents were genuinely unaware of the missing words (e.g., words only produced with other caregivers). A potential solution could be to ask all caregivers to download the app and link the accounts (if possible), or to request parents to ask other caregivers whether their child produced any of the words prompted by the app (as suggested above). Given the flexibility of the study, parents could mark words at any time during the study. However, this approach has the downside that, when it comes to analyses, only flexible statistical models such as generalized additive models can be used. This problem could be avoided by sending a reminder to parents through the app to do a vocabulary update on certain days during the study. In this way, one can guarantee that a number of data points will have the same time distance between them across participants. Another problem encountered during the audio recording collection is that some parents recorded more than one topic at the same time. To avoid topic overlapping, one could instruct parents to stop recording a topic if another of the requested topic started occurring. Also, significant time was required by the researcher to check the topic of the recording sent by the families. Valuable time could have been saved if there were a feature in the app where parents could label the audio recordings themselves with the intended topic. Finally, the present study could not validate comprehension vocabularies (i.e., words that children understand) based on recordings.
sections.text: The present work has a number of limitations. As mentioned above, parents generally failed to mark some words. A potential solution for this in future research would be to set the app to randomly ask parents whether their child knows some particular words. For example, they could be words that the child still does not produce but that, according to normative data, a child of the same age generally would. Another issue reported in this paper is that parents tend to report words in batches. Reporting individual words can be demanding. Perhaps the above solution of sending questions to parents through the app would encourage parents to enter data more often. The current research cannot confirm whether parents were genuinely unaware of the missing words (e.g., words only produced with other caregivers). A potential solution could be to ask all caregivers to download the app and link the accounts (if possible), or to request parents to ask other caregivers whether their child produced any of the words prompted by the app (as suggested above). Given the flexibility of the study, parents could mark words at any time during the study. However, this approach has the downside that, when it comes to analyses, only flexible statistical models such as generalized additive models can be used. This problem could be avoided by sending a reminder to parents through the app to do a vocabulary update on certain days during the study. In this way, one can guarantee that a number of data points will have the same time distance between them across participants. Valuable time could have been saved if there were a feature in the app where parents could label the audio recordings themselves with the intended topic. Finally, the present study could not validate comprehension vocabularies (i.e., words that children understand) based on recordings.
